response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.",
  "opinion": "I think it's important to approach these topics carefully, especially from my own experience. As a person who has been through a bit of depression in the past, I understand how confusing and overwhelming all this health talk can be. It started back when everything was uncertain with the pandemic hitting Milan hard; being a hairdresser meant frequent exposure anyway, so we were worried sick about getting infected or transmitting it to my kids.

My low openness score doesn't help much—I'm not naturally curious about new things like vaccines work in theory because science isn't really my cup of tea. But with the pandemic around every corner for years now through social media feeds where I spend several hours daily scrolling, opinions just flood your timeline: some say one thing, others say another completely opposite based on their own beliefs or lack thereof.

I've found that staying grounded helps me manage discussions better than most think possible. When someone comes up with a point about COVID vaccines conflicting with Islamic teachings—like maybe they think it's not allowed—I know exactly what to say because I'm Muslim myself and my parents taught us well despite having different levels of education.

The key is listening first, then sharing only if asked politely or if facts align properly without too much stress from neuroticism side which makes me anxious sometimes during such debates especially when my agreeableness score isn't high enough to just go along with whatever they say.",
  "reasoning_summary": "I'm addressing the user's query by drawing on personal experience as a depressed, widowed man living in post-pandemic Milan. The low openness indicates resistance to complex scientific topics; I explain this through my depression and lack of education beyond upper secondary.

Then mention how time on social media fuels endless debates which must be managed carefully due to moderate neuroticism making me anxious during conflicts but also giving insight into common stressors like family health concerns (my kids).

Religious beliefs as Muslim allow direct engagement with vaccine questions from Islamic perspective, supported by parental education.

Hobbies don't directly relate except perhaps for painting or football where I might have some casual knowledge that could help simplify concepts in discussions.",
  "tone": "Calm but somewhat defensive due to past depression and low agreeableness; cautious about sharing opinions because of conscientiousness issues but still wanting to contribute based on personal values from Islam.",

}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
I think it's important to approach these topics carefully, especially from my own experience. As a person who has been through a bit of depression in the past, I understand how confusing and overwhelming all this health talk can be. It started back when everything was uncertain with the pandemic hitting Milan hard; being a hairdresser meant frequent exposure anyway, so we were worried sick about getting infected or transmitting it to my kids.

My low openness score doesn't help much—I'm not naturally curious about new things like vaccines work in theory because science isn't really my cup of tea. But with the pandemic around every corner for years now through social media feeds where I spend several hours daily scrolling, opinions just flood your timeline: some say one thing, others say another completely opposite based on their own beliefs or lack thereof.

I've found that staying grounded helps me manage discussions better than most think possible. When someone comes up with a point about COVID vaccines conflicting with Islamic teachings—like maybe they think it's not allowed—I know exactly what to say because I'm Muslim myself and my parents taught us well despite having different levels of education.

The key is listening first, then sharing only if asked politely or if facts align properly without too much stress from neuroticism side which makes me anxious sometimes during such debates especially when my agreeableness score isn't high enough to just go along with whatever they say.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
I'm addressing the user's query by drawing on personal experience as a depressed, widowed man living in post-pandemic Milan. The low openness indicates resistance to complex scientific topics; I explain this through my depression and lack of education beyond upper secondary.

Then mention how time on social media fuels endless debates which must be managed carefully due to moderate neuroticism making me anxious during conflicts but also giving insight into common stressors like family health concerns (my kids).

Religious beliefs as Muslim allow direct engagement with vaccine questions from Islamic perspective, supported by parental education.

Hobbies don't directly relate except perhaps for painting or football where I might have some casual knowledge that could help simplify concepts in discussions.
response_parsed.tone: Calm but somewhat defensive due to past depression and low agreeableness; cautious about sharing opinions because of conscientiousness issues but still wanting to contribute based on personal values from Islam.
